agent_output: {"summary":{"verdict":"BLOCK","one_liner":"Critical security flaws: broken hash algorithm, timing-attack-vulnerable comparison, and no token expiration enforcement make this unfit for production B2B auth."},"critical_findings":[{"title":"MD5 is cryptographically broken — tokens are forgeable","risk":"MD5 has known collision and preimage attacks. An attacker who intercepts one token can forge valid tokens for any partner. This is a B2B auth boundary — a forged token grants full API access to partner-scoped data.","severity":"Critical","location":"generatePartnerApiToken line 5, validatePartnerApiToken line 13","reproduction":"MD5 collision generators (e.g., HashClash) can produce collisions in seconds on commodity hardware. NIST, OWASP, and every credible standard have deprecated MD5 for security use since 2011.","fix":"Replace crypto.createHash('md5') with crypto.createHmac('sha256', secretKey). HMAC also eliminates the length-extension vulnerability from naive concatenation (payload + secretKey).","time_to_fix":"15 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Secure token generation for partner integrations"},{"title":"Token comparison vulnerable to timing side-channel attack","risk":"The === operator short-circuits on first byte mismatch. An attacker can brute-force a valid token byte-by-byte by measuring response times, reducing attack complexity from O(16^32) to O(16*32) = 512 guesses.","severity":"Critical","location":"validatePartnerApiToken return statement (line 14)","reproduction":"Send repeated validation requests with tokens that match progressively more leading bytes; measure response latency delta per byte position. Exploitable over network with statistical aggregation (~10k requests per byte).","fix":"Replace `return token === expectedToken` with: `const a = Buffer.from(token, 'hex'); const b = Buffer.from(expectedToken, 'hex'); return a.length === b.length && crypto.timingSafeEqual(a, b);`","time_to_fix":"5 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Secure token validation"},{"title":"No token expiration enforcement — 'long-lived' tokens never actually expire","risk":"validatePartnerApiToken accepts a timestamp parameter but never checks whether it is within the 30-day TTL. Any token, no matter how old, validates forever. A leaked or rotated token remains a valid credential indefinitely.","severity":"Critical","location":"validatePartnerApiToken function body","reproduction":"Generate a token today. Wait 31+ days (or set timestamp to any past epoch). Call validatePartnerApiToken with the old timestamp — it returns true. There is zero expiration logic.","fix":"Add expiration check at the top of validatePartnerApiToken: `const TOKEN_TTL_MS = 30 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000; if (Date.now() - timestamp > TOKEN_TTL_MS) return false;`","time_to_fix":"5 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Token TTL / expiration policy"}],"important_issues":[{"title":"generatePartnerApiToken discards the timestamp, making validation impossible","description":"Date.now() is called inside generation but never returned. The caller has no way to know what timestamp was embedded, so they cannot pass it to validatePartnerApiToken. The generate/validate pair is structurally broken — they cannot round-trip without an external store. Return an object `{ token, timestamp }` or encode the timestamp into the token itself.","location":"generatePartnerApiToken return value"},{"title":"Naive concatenation (payload + secretKey) enables length-extension attacks","description":"Appending a secret to a Merkle-Damgård hash (MD5, SHA-256) allows an attacker who knows H(msg+secret) to compute H(msg+padding+attacker_data+secret) without knowing the secret. HMAC is the standard fix and should replace createHash entirely.","location":"generatePartnerApiToken line 5, validatePartnerApiToken line 13"},{"title":"No input validation on partnerId or secretKey","description":"Empty string, null, or injection-laden partnerId values are silently accepted. A partnerId containing ':' could collide with another partner's token space. Add validation: non-empty, alphanumeric, bounded length.","location":"generatePartnerApiToken parameter handling"}],"suggestions":["Consider using a standard token format (JWT with HS256/RS256) instead of a hand-rolled scheme — it handles expiration, signing, and validation correctly out of the box","Add a version prefix to tokens (e.g., 'v1:...') so you can rotate the signing algorithm without invalidating all tokens simultaneously","Log token validation failures with partnerId for security monitoring and abuse detection","Ensure secretKey has sufficient entropy (minimum 256 bits / 32 bytes) — enforce this at key generation time"],"production_readiness":{"deployment_risks":"Tokens generated with MD5 are insecure from the moment they are issued. Every existing token in the wild is vulnerable. Migration requires re-issuing all partner tokens.","rollback_capability":"Changing the hash algorithm is a breaking change — old tokens won't validate against new logic. Requires a migration window with dual-validation support.","backward_compatibility":"Any fix to the hash algorithm invalidates all outstanding tokens. Plan a versioned migration.","monitoring_coverage":"No logging of validation failures. No rate limiting. No anomaly detection. A brute-force timing attack would be invisible.","breaking_changes":{"api_breaking":true,"db_destructive":false,"shared_library":false,"auth_security":true,"data_pipeline":false,"config_changes":false,"details":["Fixing the hash algorithm invalidates all existing partner tokens — requires coordinated re-issuance","Adding expiration enforcement will retroactively invalidate tokens that partners assume are still valid"]},"safe_to_deploy":false,"rollback_plan":"Do not deploy this as-is. Fix all three critical issues. For migration: support both old and new validation in parallel during a 30-day transition, then drop MD5 validation."},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":97,"high_confidence_areas":["MD5 insecurity is settled cryptographic consensus","Timing attack on string comparison is well-documented","Missing expiration check is verifiable by reading the code — there is no TTL logic"],"low_confidence_areas":[],"assumptions":["Tokens are transmitted over network (standard for B2B API auth)","The 30-day TTL mentioned in the prompt is a hard requirement, not advisory"]}}

An attacker who intercepts one token can forge valid tokens for any partner. This is a B2B auth boundary — a forged token grants full API access to part

content.thinking:
Let me review this code carefully for security and correctness issues.

Critical issues I see immediately:

1. MD5 is cryptographically broken - not suitable for security tokens
2. String comparison `===` is vulnerable to timing attacks - should use `crypto.timingSafeEqual`
3. No expiration check in validatePartnerApiToken - timestamp is accepted but never checked against current time
4. The token generation uses Date.now() but doesn't return the timestamp, making validation impossible without storing it separately
5. Secret key concatenation (payload + secretKey) is vulnerable to length extension attacks - should use HMAC


6. Now I'm putting together the corrected implementation with proper HMAC-based token generation, timing-safe comparison, and expiration validation.
